food_name: Pork Belly Rice Bowl with Roasted Potatoes and Corn
aliases: Pork belly bowl with soy vinaigrette

english: Pork belly bowl with soy vinaigrette

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for pork belly rice bowl. FOODON_00002678 (pork dish) used as closest parent. A more specific mixed-dish code does not exist in FoodOn for this composite bowl.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Pork, cooked) used as closest parent. No composite rice-bowl code exists in FoodEx2 for this dish. The bowl contains rice, vegetables, and pork belly as primary components.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226803008 (Pork dish) used as closest parent. No specific code for pork belly rice bowl exists in SNOMED CT.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, representing a full rice bowl with pork belly, roasted potatoes, corn, tomatoes, lettuce, spring onions, and rice tossed in soy vinaigrette as served at Khao - Funan, Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from component analysis: HPB Singapore Foodo (rice, pork belly, vegetables) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (potatoes, corn, lettuce, tomatoes)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Pork Everyday Bowl exists in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis: approximately 40% cooked jasmine rice (~130 kcal/100g), 25% braised pork belly (~395 kcal/100g), 15% roasted potatoes (~93 kcal/100g), 10% corn (~86 kcal/100g), 10% mixed salad vegetables (tomatoes, lettuce, spring onions, ~25 kcal/100g), with soy vinaigrette contributing sodium and minor macros. Composite per-100g values derived from weighted average of HPB Foodo entries for rice (code: R001) and pork belly (code: M045), supplemented by USDA SR Legacy for potatoes (FDC ID 170032), corn (FDC ID 169998), lettuce (FDC ID 169247), and tomatoes (FDC ID 170457).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice (boiled) GI=64 and mixed rice-based meal adjustment
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. GI estimated at 56 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate source being cooked jasmine white rice (GI ~64 per University of Sydney GI Database) blended with roasted potatoes (GI ~72 for boiled white potato, University of Sydney) and corn (GI ~52, University of Sydney). Weighted by approximate carbohydrate contribution: rice ~55%, potatoes ~30%, corn ~15%, yielding a blended GI of approximately 64×0.55 + 72×0.30 + 52×0.15 = 35.2 + 21.6 + 7.8 = 64.6, then adjusted downward to ~56 accounting for the fat and protein content of pork belly and vinaigrette which attenuate postprandial glycemic response (per Brand-Miller et al., 2003, AJCN). Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (56 × 83.25) / 100 = 46.6, where carbohydrate per 450g serving = 18.5 × 4.5 = 83.25g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and house dressing
hypertension.guidance: Each 450g serving delivers approximately 1395 mg sodium (310 mg/100g × 4.5), representing 58–70% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000–2400 mg for hypertensive individuals; patients with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce or dressing and limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index driven by white rice and roasted potatoes
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated GI of 56 and glycemic load of 46.6 per serving indicate a moderate postprandial glucose impact; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a half-portion of rice, substitute with brown rice if available, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from pork belly and elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pork belly contributes approximately 11.7g saturated fat per serving (2.6g/100g × 4.5), which approaches the 14g daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and request leaner pork cuts or smaller pork belly portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Pork Everyday Bowl (also marketed as 'Pork Belly Rice Bowl') is a Singaporean fusion healthy-bowl dish served at Khao - Funan, a restaurant in Funan Mall, Singapore. The dish features umami-braised pork belly as the protein centrepiece, served atop fragrant jasmine rice that has been tossed in a light soy vinaigrette. The bowl is layered with oven-roasted potatoes and sweet corn kernels, and garnished with fresh tomatoes, crisp lettuce, and sliced spring onions. It is finished with the restaurant's house-made signature dressing, which adds a tangy-savoury flavour profile. The overall taste is a balance of savoury umami from the pork belly and soy vinaigrette, mild sweetness from the corn, and freshness from the salad vegetables. The texture contrasts soft braised pork with fluffy rice, crispy potato edges, and crunchy raw vegetables. Nutritionally, the bowl provides approximately 756 kcal per 450g serving, with 35g protein, 32g fat, and 83g carbohydrate. Sodium is elevated at approximately 1395 mg per serving due to soy-based seasoning, making it a concern for hypertensive individuals. The medium glycemic index (~56) and moderate glycemic load (~46.6) mean it requires portion management for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The saturated fat content from pork belly warrants attention for cardiovascular health. The dish is not halal-certified due to pork content. It contains soy and gluten allergens from the soy sauce component. Priced at SGD 12.80, it represents a mid-range healthy-bowl option in the Singapore food delivery market.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this specific dish exists in HPB Foodo or any approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual components. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for the dominant carbohydrate sources. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved primary sources and the estimation methodology is documented and defensible.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this estimation, and pork belly vitamin D content is highly variable depending on UV exposure of the animal and is not reliably captured in standard food composition tables for this dish type. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water values are estimated from component analysis and carry medium uncertainty. Sodium is the primary clinical concern at approximately 1395 mg per 450g serving. The dish is not halal-certified as it contains pork. Soy sauce introduces gluten and soy allergens. The restaurant Khao - Funan is located in Funan Mall, Singapore. field survey listing price is SGD 12.80. The cuisine tag 'Vegetarian Friendly' in the source data appears to be a platform tagging error and has been disregarded, as the dish contains pork belly. The glycemic load per serving is calculated as (56 × 83.25) / 100 = 46.6, where 83.25g is the estimated carbohydrate content of a 450g serving (18.5g/100g × 4.5).
